---
title: "Kinetics-as-data refactor — audit and implementation plan (issue #32)"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-22"
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
---

*Updated 2026-05-23: added §"Beyond Level 1" — the four-level spectrum
toward fully data-driven, with a worked `reactions.yaml` sketch for the
current model and a candid audit of what resists data-fication.*

# Goal

Move all numerical model data out of Python source and into structured,
human-reviewable files. Currently `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/`
mixes ~150 numerical constants in with the ODE logic, which makes
parameter review hard for biologist collaborators and obscures the
data-vs-algorithm boundary that the rest of the architecture honours.

This is the audit and implementation plan for issue
[#32](https://github.com/stevehaigh/platelet-wcm/issues/32). It also
introduces the byte-identical regression test suite (committed in this
session) that brackets the refactor: any externalisation step that is
supposed to be behaviour-preserving will be verified bit-for-bit.

# Audit findings

## Numerical constants by file

| File | What | Count |
|------|------|-------|
| `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/calcium_signalling.py` | `K_*` rate-constant dicts | 19 |
| `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/calcium_signalling.py` | Module-level scalar constants (`CA_EX_UM`, `V_CYT_L`, `N_GSN`, conductances, time constants) | ~30 |
| `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/internal_state.py` | `_MOLECULES` tuple list (id × mass_fg × initial_count × class) | 63 rows |
| `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/resting_decay.py` | `_PROTEIN_HALF_LIFE_SEC` | 1 |

Roughly **115 named scalars + 19 dicts + 63-row species table** —
consistent with the issue's "~150–200" estimate.

## Sub-areas requiring design choices

These are not blockers, but each one bends the simplest "pure data
file" picture and needs a per-case answer in the schema:

**Computed defaults**
:   e.g. `_UM_PER_COUNT_CYT = 1.0 / (N_A * V_CYT_L * 1e-6)` is derived
    from three other constants. Options: pre-compute and bake into the
    TOML (loses the derivation), or keep a small builder function that
    fills derived fields at load. Recommended: builder, because the
    derivation captures provenance that's meaningful for review.

**Cross-referenced counts**
:   `N_HSP90B1_M = N_HSP90B1 * 4` (sites per molecule). Same fix:
    TOML carries the inputs (`molecules = 10_000`, `sites_per_molecule_medium = 4`),
    the loader computes the product.

**Module-mutation overrides**
:   `cs_mod.CA_EX_UM = X` is the current Phase-3 condition-switch
    mechanism (set by `run_platelet_sim` before sim construction). After
    externalisation, the override needs either a config-object pattern
    (passed through to the solver) or a transition shim that still
    mutates a module-level attribute the loader reads. Recommended: keep
    a thin shim during migration, then plumb a `SimulationParams`
    dataclass through the engine in a separate follow-up.

**Calibration-coupled comments**
:   ~5 places in `calcium_signalling.py` have multi-paragraph provenance
    comments adjacent to a single constant (e.g. the `GAMMA_IP3R_S`
    block walks through the SERCA-balanced derivation). TOML comments
    can carry one-line provenance but can't host paragraphs. Option:
    move the prose to `reports/dissertation-notes.md` and reference by
    section number in a TOML comment; preserve the value + provenance
    relationship via a deliberate link.

**Process-coupled dicts**
:   `MOLECULE_NAMES` tuple, `_IDX` dict, and the embedded species
    ordering in `_ode_rhs` are tightly coupled — the integer indices
    must match what the listener reads. The refactor needs a shared
    "species ordering" source-of-truth, not duplicate definitions in
    the TOML and the Python.

# Target schema

## Kinetic parameters — TOML

Single file `reports/params/calcium-v0.5.toml` (versioned to model
release). One `[table]` per rate-constant group, with the existing
dict key names preserved verbatim so the loader-to-engine mapping is
trivial.

```toml
[meta]
version = "v0.5"
last_updated = "2026-05-22"
note = "First externalised release; matches calcium_signalling.py @ 9f253e42"

[volumes]
V_CYT_L = 6.0e-15      # cytosol (Purvis 2008)
V_DTS_L = 0.258e-15    # 4.3% of cytosol; same source

[physical]
N_A = 6.022e23
F_FARADAY = 96485.0
R_GAS = 8.314
T_KELVIN = 310.0       # 37°C; Purvis/Dolan
V_IM_V = -0.060
V_PM_V = -0.060

[ip3r]
# deYoung-Keizer 1992 / Li-Rinzel 1994
d1 = 0.13              # IP3 activation half-saturation (µM)
d2 = 1.049             # Ca²⁺ inhibition half-saturation (µM)
d5 = 0.08234           # Ca²⁺ activation half-saturation (µM)
a2 = 0.2               # Ca²⁺ inhibition on-rate (µM⁻¹·s⁻¹)
N_channels = 1328      # Burkhart 2012; Dolan Table S1
gamma_pS = 0.075       # calibrated, see dissertation-notes §3.1
source = "deYoung & Keizer 1992 PNAS 89:9895; Li & Rinzel 1994 JTB 166:461"

[serca]
k_shuttle_f = 600.0
k_shuttle_r = 600.0
k_bind_f = 210.0
# ... etc

[par1]
k_cleave = 2.0
k_internalize = 0.02
N_total = 2500
source = "Coller 1995"
```

One `[table]` per `K_*` dict; loader's job is to expose them in a way
that the engine code can consume drop-in (i.e. `K_SERCA['k_bind_f']`
still works after the refactor — see Loader architecture below).

## Initial conditions — TSV

`reports/params/species-v0.5.tsv`. 63 rows, four canonical columns plus
optional commentary:

```
id                    mass_fg     initial_count   class       notes
CA2_CYT[c]            6.660e-8    361             metabolite  100 nM × 6 fL
CA2_DTS[dts]          6.660e-8    38842           metabolite  250 µM × 4.3% × 6 fL
IP3R_h[dts]           5.110e-4    1212            protein     h₀ = 0.913 × N_IP3R
...
```

Lives alongside the kinetics TOML so a parameter set is a directory.
TSV (not CSV) because tabs separate cleanly through molecule IDs
containing brackets and dots.

# Loader architecture

## Where the loaders live

```
reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/
  ├── process/
  │   ├── calcium_signalling.py   # consumes loader output; algorithm only
  │   ├── resting_decay.py        # ditto
  │   └── _params_loader.py       # NEW — reads TOML, returns dicts
  └── internal_state.py           # consumes species TSV
```

## Public API

`_params_loader.py` exposes one function:

```python
def load_calcium_params(toml_path: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Read a kinetics TOML, fill derived fields, return a flat dict
    keyed by the names the engine currently uses (`K_SERCA`, `CA_EX_UM`,
    `N_HSP90B1_M`, …). Drop-in replacement for the current module-level
    bindings."""
```

The engine module then becomes a thin wrapper:

```python
# calcium_signalling.py top of file:
_PARAMS_PATH = os.environ.get(
    'PLATELET_PARAMS', 'reports/params/calcium-v0.5.toml')
_p = load_calcium_params(_PARAMS_PATH)
K_SERCA = _p['K_SERCA']
K_PMCA = _p['K_PMCA']
CA_EX_UM = _p['CA_EX_UM']
# ... etc
```

After the migration the engine module is mostly the ODE itself plus
algorithm constants (`ORAI_SUBUNITS_PER_CHANNEL = 4` is arguably more
"definition" than "data" — keep in code? open question).

## Multi-scenario via `--params`

```bash
python runscripts/manual/runPlateletSim.py out/sweep \
    --params reports/params/dolan-2014.toml
```

`runPlateletSim.py` reads `--params` and either passes the path to
`load_calcium_params` directly, or sets the env var the engine uses.
Either works; env var is simpler.

# Migration plan

Phased so each step is independently verifiable against the byte-
identical regression suite (already landed today).

| Phase | Effort | Risk | Reversible |
|-------|--------|------|-----------|
| **0**. Byte-identical regression suite ← **done** today | 1 h | none | n/a |
| **1**. Choose format + design schema (this doc) | done | none | n/a |
| **2**. Build `_params_loader.py` + write the first TOML | half-day | low | trivial revert |
| **3**. Refactor `calcium_signalling.py` to consume loader output | half-day | **medium** | trivial revert |
| **4**. Byte-identical regression test passes against the externalised version | half-day | this is where the actual risk lives | — |
| **5**. Refactor `internal_state.py` to consume species TSV | 1 h | low | trivial revert |
| **6**. Add `--params` CLI flag + multi-scenario plumbing | 1 h | low | additive |
| **7**. Documentation: schema reference + how to add a new parameter | 1 h | low | additive |

Total **~2 days** after the regression suite is in. The regression
suite is the unblock: without it the medium-risk phase 3 step is
unverifiable.

## Recommended sequencing

- **Now** (today): phase 0 — byte-identical regression suite. ✓
- **Now or next session**: phase 1 (this doc). ✓
- **Defer** (post-dissertation, per the original framing in #32):
  phases 2–7. Reason: the medium-risk step (phase 3) carries real
  refactor risk during writing; a 1% drift in resting Ca²⁺ during
  dissertation finalisation is exactly the kind of obstacle worth
  avoiding.
- **Or, if appetite during dev runway** (4–6 weeks before writeup):
  phases 2–4 on a feature branch, merged only when the byte-identical
  test is green. Phases 5–7 follow as small independent commits.

# Regression test strategy

## What's already in place (today)

- **Range-based tests** (`models/platelet/tests/sim/test_regression.py`)
  — assert resting Ca²⁺ ∈ [80, 120] nM, peak ∈ [200, 800] nM, etc. Good
  guardrails, but pass under FP-order drift.
- **Phase 3 acceptance criteria** — 5/5 specific biology assertions on
  peak heights and SOCE differential. Same range-based.
- **Smoke tests** — dose sweep, calcium signalling kwargs, etc.

These continue to be the right safety net for *biological* drift —
they tell you when the model has moved off-target.

## What landed today

`models/platelet/tests/sim/test_byte_identical.py` — **bit-for-bit
regression** on two deterministic scenarios:

1. **`default_activation_30s`** — Phase 3 +Ca²⁺ condition, 30 s,
   seed 0. Exercises the full GPCR cascade → IP3 → IP3R → DTS-release
   → SOCE → PMCA pathway.
2. **`at_rest_30s`** — all three agonist peaks set to 0, 30 s, seed 0.
   Exercises the resting fixed point.

For each scenario the test reads four CalciumTrace columns
(`ca_cyt_nM`, `ca_dts_uM`, `ip3_nM`, `soce_flux_nMs`) and compares
them against a golden NPZ via `np.testing.assert_array_equal`. The
golden files live under `models/platelet/tests/sim/golden/` and are
~2 KB each.

If a refactor drifts the output:

```bash
$ pytest models/platelet/tests/sim/test_byte_identical.py
…
AssertionError: Byte-identical regression in scenario 'default_activation_30s',
column 'ca_cyt_nM'. If this drift is expected (e.g. you intentionally
changed kinetics), regenerate with REGEN_GOLDEN=1.
```

The failing run's NPZ can be saved next to the golden and `diff`-ed in
Python — every sample at every timestep is available.

## Why this is the right tool for #32

The kinetics-as-data refactor is the canonical case of "should be
behaviour-preserving". Any FP-order shift (e.g. dict iteration order
changing in a Python upgrade, a TOML float being parsed differently
from a literal in source) will be caught. The cost of false positives
is bounded — if a library upgrade legitimately drifts the output, the
fix is one env-var-prefixed test re-run plus a commit of the new NPZ.

## What it does **not** cover

- **Stochastic processes** — currently none in the model. If
  `runPlateletSim.py --seed N` becomes meaningful later, the byte-
  identical scope will need expanding.
- **Long-horizon drift** — 30 s scenarios catch the spike and the
  early recovery. If a refactor only affects the slow approach to
  steady-state at t > 200 s, byte-identical at 30 s won't catch it.
  Range-based Phase 3 tests at t = 200 s are the safety net there.
- **Platform sensitivity** — byte-identical is per-platform.
  Macbook + pyenv 3.11.5 + scipy 1.x is the current baseline; CI
  failures on different platforms aren't necessarily bugs.

# Open questions

1. **Algorithm constants vs data constants.** Some module-level scalars
   are arguably algorithm (e.g. `ORAI_SUBUNITS_PER_CHANNEL = 4`,
   `STIM_MONOMERS_PER_DIMER = 2` — these define stoichiometry, not
   kinetics). Should they stay in code? Recommendation: yes, with a
   clear comment block. The TOML is for kinetics and inventory; the
   code holds the stoichiometric / topological structure.

2. **Computed defaults at load vs. at use.** `_UM_PER_COUNT_CYT` is
   used inside the ODE on every step. If computed at load time and
   baked into the engine module's globals, no perf cost. If computed
   inside the ODE on every call, small perf cost. Recommendation:
   load-time, matching current behaviour.

3. **Parameter-set provenance.** Each TOML should declare which git
   SHA of the engine it was tested against, and the loader should
   warn (not error) if the engine has moved on. Trivial to implement;
   useful for the multi-scenario use case.

4. **TS/React UI** (the other half of #32) is unaffected by this
   audit. Once parameters are externalised the UI can consume them
   directly; the data layer doesn't care which UI is on top of it.

# Beyond Level 1 — toward fully data-driven (Levels 2–4)

The migration plan above takes the model to **Level 1**: parameters
externalised, code still owns the structure of every reaction. That's
the right first cut. This section asks the harder question — could the
model be driven *purely* by data, with the Python source containing
nothing but a rate-law function library and an ODE runner?

The honest answer is "almost, with named exceptions". The platelet
model uses a small library of rate-law forms across roughly forty
reactions; that's exactly the shape that data-driven biology
frameworks are built for. But three or four specific mechanisms
(IP3R inactivation as a dynamical variable, the MWC fixed-point in
SOCE, conditional Nernst-flux guards, agonist forcing curves) carry
just enough algorithmic content that pushing them into pure data
either forces a vocabulary expansion (SBML rate rules, events) or
moves complexity from one file to another without a real win. The
pragmatic target is Level 2 (reactions-as-data, with a registered
rate-law function library), not Level 3 (full SBML).

## The four-level spectrum

| Level | What moves to data | Code still owns | Effort | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Numerical params** (this doc, §"Migration plan") | The ~115 scalars + 19 `K_*` dicts + 63-row species table | All reaction connectivity, rate-law forms, ODE wiring | ~2 days | low; byte-identical achievable via the regression suite |
| **2. Reactions-as-data** | Reaction connectivity + stoichiometry + per-reaction rate-law selection + initial conditions + compartment table + forcing-curve parameters | A library of rate-law *functions* (mass-action, Hill, Nernst-flux, deYoung-Keizer, MWC), the ODE runner, the conditional-flux guards | ~1–2 weeks | medium; partial byte-identical; reaction-by-reaction porting needed |
| **3. Full SBML / Antimony** | Everything Level 2 covers, plus standard-format compatibility with the broader systems-biology tooling | A thin adapter to libsbml/tellurium, plus any rate laws SBML can't express directly (e.g. MWC iterative solver) | weeks | high; some rate laws don't map cleanly; need explicit "event" or "algebraic constraint" semantics |
| **4. Rule-based (BioNetGen / Kappa)** | Species become agents with internal state patterns; reactions become rules that match those patterns | The rule engine, the simulator. Most of the platelet code becomes obsolete | months | paradigm shift; over-engineered for ~40 reactions |

Each level is strictly a superset of the one above it in expressive
power, and strictly a subset in "what the host code knows about". A
team typically lands on Level 2 because Level 3 forces every rate law
into the SBML vocabulary, and Level 4 is for combinatorially explosive
state spaces (TCR signalling, immune receptor assembly) that we don't
have here.

## Level 2 — what the input data would look like

Six files per parameter set. A directory under `reports/params/`
becomes one full model specification:

```
reports/params/v0.5/
  meta.toml            version, last_updated, engine SHA, notes
  params.toml          the 115 scalars + 19 K_* groups from Level 1
  compartments.tsv     id, name, volume, membrane potential (where defined)
  species.tsv          id, mass_fg, initial_count, class, compartment, notes
  reactions.yaml       one entry per reaction; selects a rate law by name
  forcing.yaml         parameterised time-dependent inputs (agonist curves)
  observables.yaml     which listener columns to record (optional)
```

### `compartments.tsv`

```
id   name              volume_L      V_membrane_V   notes
c    cytosol           6.00e-15      -              Purvis 2008
dts  dense-tubular     2.58e-16      -0.060         4.3% of cytosol; Dolan 2014
pl   plasmalemma       -             -0.060         membrane potential only
m    mitochondrial     0.05e-15      -              issue #22 scope
e    extracellular     ∞             -              infinite reservoir
```

### `species.tsv`

(Already partly tabular as `_MOLECULES` in `internal_state.py`. 63 rows;
truncated example.)

```
id                  mass_fg     initial_count   class       compartment   notes
CA2_CYT[c]          6.660e-8    361             ion         c             100 nM × 6 fL
CA2_DTS[dts]        6.660e-8    38842           ion         dts           250 µM × 4.3% × 6 fL
IP3R_h[dts]         0.0         1212            dyn_var     dts           h dimensionless, scaled by N_IP3R
SERCA_E1[dts]       1.10e-4     5946            protein     dts           half of 11892 SERCA at t=0
PIP2[c]             8.34e-7     1120000         lipid       c             Mazet 2020
…
```

Note the new `class` tag — `ion / protein / lipid / dyn_var` so the
loader can tell e.g. "do not include `IP3R_h` in mass conservation
checks; it's a dimensionless dynamical state".

### `reactions.yaml` — the new piece

Each reaction names its rate-law form, lists reactants and products
with stoichiometry, and references parameters by dotted path into
`params.toml`. Examples covering the variety of rate-law shapes the
current model actually uses:

```yaml
# ── Mass-action, reversible ───────────────────────────────────────────
- id: calr_buffering
  description: Calreticulin C-domain low-affinity Ca²⁺ buffering (Phase 2)
  rate_law: mass_action
  reactants: [{species: CALR_free[dts], n: 1}, {species: CA2_DTS[dts], n: 1}]
  products:  [{species: CALR_Ca[dts],   n: 1}]
  reversible: true
  parameters:
    k_forward: calr.k_on
    k_reverse: calr.k_off

# ── Mass-action, irreversible, time-dependent input ──────────────────
- id: par1_cleavage
  description: PAR1 proteolytic cleavage by thrombin (fast, irreversible)
  rate_law: mass_action
  reactants: [{species: PAR1_inactive[pl], n: 1}]
  modifiers: [{forcing: thrombin}]            # see forcing.yaml
  products:  [{species: PAR1_active[pl],   n: 1}]
  reversible: false
  parameters:
    k_forward: par1.k_cleave

# ── Cycle step — multi-Ca²⁺ binding with mass-action n=2 ─────────────
- id: serca_bind
  description: E1 + 2 Ca²⁺_cyt ⇌ E1·Ca₂  (Purvis 2008 / Dode 2002)
  rate_law: mass_action
  reactants: [{species: SERCA_E1[dts], n: 1},
              {species: CA2_CYT[c],    n: 2}]
  products:  [{species: SERCA_E1Ca[dts], n: 1}]
  reversible: true
  parameters: {k_forward: serca.k_bind_f, k_reverse: serca.k_bind_r}

# ── Catalytic enzyme step — substrate consumed, enzyme conserved ─────
- id: plcb_pip2_hydrolysis
  description: PIP2 → IP3 + DAG catalysed by active PLCβ (Phase 4 / #31)
  rate_law: catalytic_first_order
  catalyst:  {species: PLCb_active[c]}
  reactants: [{species: PIP2[c], n: 1}]
  products:  [{species: IP3[c], n: 1}, {species: DAG[c], n: 1}]
  parameters: {k_cat: plcb.k_cat}

# ── Nernst-form Ca²⁺ current, gated by a Po expression ──────────────
- id: ip3r_flux
  description: IP3R Ca²⁺ release into cyt (Dolan 2014 eq. 4)
  rate_law: nernst_current_gated
  source_pool: {species: CA2_DTS[dts]}
  sink_pool:   {species: CA2_CYT[c]}
  membrane_potential: ip3r.V_membrane_V       # = V_IM_V
  gating:
    expression: ip3r.de_young_keizer          # named function in code
    inputs: [IP3[c], CA2_CYT[c], IP3R_h[dts]]
  parameters:
    gamma_conductance: ip3r.gamma_pS
    channel_count: ip3r.N_channels
  conditional: [CA2_CYT[c] > 0, CA2_DTS[dts] > 0]

# ── Rate-rule (dynamical variable, not a species count) ─────────────
- id: ip3r_h_dynamics
  description: deYoung-Keizer slow Ca²⁺ inactivation of IP3R (Li-Rinzel 1994)
  rate_law: rate_rule                          # dy/dt = expr; not a reaction
  variable: IP3R_h[dts]
  expression: ip3r.dh_dt_de_young_keizer       # named function
  inputs: [CA2_CYT[c], IP3R_h[dts]]
  parameters: {a2: ip3r.a2, d2: ip3r.d2}

# ── Hill kinetics ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
- id: mcu_uptake
  description: MCU mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uniporter (cooperative; issue #22)
  rate_law: hill
  source_pool: {species: CA2_CYT[c]}
  sink_pool:   {species: CA2_MITO[m]}
  parameters:
    V_max: mito.V_max_MCU
    K_half: mito.K_MCU
    n: mito.n_MCU

# ── MWC fixed-point + Nernst current (SOCE) ─────────────────────────
- id: soce_flux
  description: Store-operated Ca²⁺ entry via Orai (Dolan 2014 MWC scheme)
  rate_law: mwc_soce_current                   # custom; see rate-law library
  source_pool: extracellular_ca                # CA_EX_UM constant
  sink_pool:   {species: CA2_CYT[c]}
  inputs: [STIM1_dim[dts], ORAI1[pl], CA2_CYT[c]]
  parameters:
    gamma_conductance: soc.gamma_pS
    membrane_potential: soc.V_membrane_V
    mwc: soc.mwc                                # L, Ka, f, a constants block
    puncta: soc.puncta                          # alpha, KM, n, baseline
  conditional: [CA_EX_UM > 0]
```

A complete `reactions.yaml` for the current model has roughly **40
entries** — three or four per pathway (GPCR cascade, PI cycle, SERCA
cycle, PMCA basal, PMCA CaM-coupled, CaM Ca²⁺ binding, STIM1, SOCE,
mitochondria, six DTS buffers, gelsolin proxy, P2X1 cycle, NCX, PM
leak), each looking like one of the seven shapes above.

### `forcing.yaml`

The plateau-decay shape is shared by all three agonists. Parameterise
once, reference from the reactions:

```yaml
- id: thrombin
  description: Thrombin time course at PAR1/PAR4 (Dolan 2014 protocol)
  shape: plateau_decay
  parameters:
    rest:      0.0          # nM
    peak:      1.0          # nM
    tau_rise:  0.5          # s
    t_peak:    5.0          # s
    tau_decay: 120.0        # s
    delay:     0.0          # s
  unit: nM

- id: adp
  shape: plateau_decay
  parameters:
    rest:      0.0
    peak:      10.0
    tau_rise:  1.0
    t_peak:    5.0
    tau_decay: 30.0
    delay:     0.0
  unit: µM

- id: atp_ex
  shape: plateau_decay
  parameters:
    rest:      0.0
    peak:      10.0
    tau_rise:  0.5
    t_peak:    1.0
    tau_decay: 30.0
    delay:     0.0
  unit: µM
```

### `observables.yaml`

Listener columns and their source. Mostly direct species reads, but
also some derived quantities (concentrations from counts, ratios,
fluxes). Optional — sensible defaults from a "record every species
count + every reaction flux" fallback.

```yaml
- id: ca_cyt_nM
  derived_from: {species: CA2_CYT[c]}
  transform: count_to_concentration_nM
  compartment: c

- id: soce_flux_nMs
  derived_from: {reaction: soce_flux}
  transform: ions_per_second_to_nM_per_second
  compartment: c
```

## The rate-law function library — code, not data

A small Python module holds the actual mathematical forms. Reactions
in `reactions.yaml` select from this library by name; adding a new
rate law means writing a new Python function and registering it.

Sketch:

```python
# reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/rate_laws.py

@register('mass_action')
def mass_action(reactants, products, k_forward, k_reverse=None, modifiers=None):
    """Standard reversible mass-action: v = k_f · ∏[reactants]^n_i  − k_r · ∏[products]^n_j"""
    ...

@register('catalytic_first_order')
def catalytic_first_order(catalyst, reactants, products, k_cat):
    """Enzyme conserved: v = k_cat · [E] · [S]"""
    ...

@register('hill')
def hill(source_pool, sink_pool, V_max, K_half, n):
    """Cooperative substrate kinetics: v = V_max · S^n / (K^n + S^n)"""
    ...

@register('nernst_current_gated')
def nernst_current_gated(source_pool, sink_pool, membrane_potential,
        gating, gamma_conductance, channel_count, conditional):
    """Po-gated Nernst Ca²⁺ current. Returns ions/s into sink_pool,
    out of source_pool. `gating` is a function of state variables
    that returns Po ∈ [0, 1]."""
    ...

@register('rate_rule')
def rate_rule(variable, expression, inputs, parameters):
    """A pure dy/dt for a non-species dynamical variable (e.g. IP3R h)."""
    ...

@register('mwc_soce_current')
def mwc_soce_current(...):
    """MWC fixed-point solver + Nernst current. Special-case for SOCE."""
    ...
```

The runner walks `reactions.yaml`, looks up the named rate law,
computes the per-reaction flux from the current state, and assembles
`dy/dt` for each species by summing fluxes weighted by stoichiometry.
Conditional guards (the `conditional` list in a reaction) become
boolean checks on inputs before computing flux.

## What resists data-fication, and why

Four specific mechanisms aren't cleanly expressible as "(reactants,
products, rate-law-name, parameters)" tuples. They need either
custom-named rate laws (Level 2) or vocabulary expansion (Level 3).

### IP3R h-variable dynamics

`IP3R_h` isn't a molecular species — it's a *dimensionless dynamical
state* of the IP3R channel ensemble (h ∈ [0, 1] is the fraction
non-inhibited; we carry it as a count for engine compatibility).
Treating it as a species creates conceptual confusion: it has no
mass, no compartment in any meaningful sense, no conservation law it
participates in. The cleanest handling is to declare it as a
`dyn_var` in `species.tsv` (excluded from mass-conservation checks)
and use a `rate_rule` reaction-shape that says "compute `dh/dt` from
this named function". SBML calls this an "assignment rule" /
"rate rule". Level 2 supports it via the dedicated `rate_rule`
rate-law shape shown above.

### MWC fixed-point in SOCE

`_mwc_open_fraction()` is an iterative Newton-style fixed-point
solver embedded in the RHS. It returns `Po`, the open-channel
probability, given STIM2 dimer count and Orai channel count. SBML's
standard rate-law forms (mass-action, Henri-Michaelis-Menten,
saturating, etc.) can't express this — you'd need either an
"algebraic constraint" SBML feature (rarely used, poorly supported)
or a custom function. Easier path: keep the MWC solver in the Python
rate-law library, register it as `mwc_soce_current`, and let the
`reactions.yaml` entry select it by name. The MWC *parameters* (L,
Ka, f, a, alpha, KM, n) move to `params.toml`; the MWC *algorithm*
stays in code.

### Conditional Nernst-flux guards

`if ca_cyt > 0 and ca_dts > 0: nernst_flux else: 0`. These guards are
there because `log(0)` is undefined and the Nernst equation needs both
concentrations positive. Express in data via a `conditional` list on
the reaction entry (as shown in the `ip3r_flux` example above), and
have the rate-law function honour it. SBML's "event" mechanism could
also encode this but is overkill — a boolean check is plenty.

### Time-dependent forcing functions

Agonist concentrations (thrombin, ADP, ATP_ex) are analytic
plateau-decay curves, not species. They drive reactions as
*modifiers*. Two ways to express in data:

1. **Parameterised curves** (shown in `forcing.yaml` above). The
   shape (`plateau_decay`) is named; the parameters are data. New
   shapes require a new function in the forcing library.
2. **Tabulated CSV** for arbitrary measured / imported curves. Slower
   (linear interpolation in the RHS) but expressively complete. SBML's
   "interpolation function" feature covers this.

For the dose-sweep work (#45) the parameterised form is exactly right
— sweeps vary the parameters, not the shape. The tabulated form would
matter if you ever wanted to drive the model with a real time-course
trace from an experiment.

### Calibration-coupled derivations

`GAMMA_IP3R_S` is *derived* from SERCA cycle steady-state flux — it's
not a measured parameter, it's a calibration anchor. Three options:

1. Bake the derived value into `params.toml` and lose the derivation.
2. Add a `derived_parameters` section to `params.toml` whose values are
   computed at load time from a small set of expressions.
3. Keep the derivation as a Python builder function called by the
   loader.

Option 3 is the safest — derivations are algorithm, not data — and
matches the discipline of keeping rate-law functions in code.
Document the derivation in a comment + the params.toml entry's
`notes` field.

## Level 3 — full SBML / Antimony

For completeness. Adoption would buy you:

- Compatibility with the broader systems-biology toolchain
  (COPASI, Tellurium, the BioModels database, JWS Online, …).
- The SBML community's standard rate-law library, which covers
  ~95% of what we need (mass-action, Hill, Michaelis-Menten,
  Goldbeter-Koshland, Henri-Michaelis-Menten, …).
- Free things like model export, parameter-fitting tooling, sensitivity
  analysis, automated graph layout.

It would cost you:

- Re-expressing the custom rate laws (MWC iterative solver, the IP3R
  Nernst-Po gated current, the deYoung-Keizer rate rule) either as
  SBML "events"/"rules" or as custom-function extensions, which
  loses the cleanliness of the standard form.
- A heavier runtime dependency (libsbml + libsedml + Tellurium ≈
  100 MB and a stiff C++ build).
- More indirection on debugging: the model exists in SBML, the engine
  is `tellurium.RoadRunner`, the rate laws are SBML expressions —
  three layers between you and a working numerical answer.

Verdict: Level 3 makes sense if and only if external interoperability
becomes a real requirement (e.g. publishing the model into BioModels,
or running it under COPASI for parameter fitting). Until then, Level 2
gives you the architectural clarity without the dependency cost.

## Level 4 — rule-based (BioNetGen, Kappa)

Briefly, for completeness. Rule-based models become useful when the
combinatorial state space is too large for explicit species
enumeration — TCR signalling with N phosphorylation sites needs 2^N
"species" in a conventional model, which is infeasible above N ≈ 5.

The platelet model doesn't have this problem. PAR1 has three states
(inactive / active / internalised); the SERCA cycle has six; the
PMCA cycle has five; CaM has three. The largest single species set
(the GPCR cascade × Gq pool) is in the hundreds. A rule-based engine
would add complexity without any combinatorial saving. **Not
recommended** for this model.

If a future extension brings in receptor multimerisation with many
sites (e.g. integrin αIIbβ3 cluster states, or platelet contact
patterns in a multi-cell model), Level 4 becomes the right answer
for *that part* — and SBML-multi or BNGL exist for exactly that
hybrid case.

## What changes vs Level 1 in terms of acceptance criteria

Level 1's acceptance is byte-identical Phase 3 output against the
golden NPZ regression suite landed today. Level 2 cannot promise that
strictly — the order of reactions added to `dy/dt` depends on the
order they appear in `reactions.yaml`, and floating-point addition is
non-associative. Three options:

1. **Relax byte-identical to "within float-error of golden"** — change
   the golden tests to `np.testing.assert_allclose(rtol=1e-9)` or
   similar. The Phase 3 acceptance criteria (range-based, ±30 % of
   Dolan peaks) are unaffected.
2. **Order the reactions in `reactions.yaml` exactly as they appear in
   the current `_ode_rhs`** — preserves FP ordering, makes
   byte-identical possible. Costs YAML ergonomics (the order isn't
   biologically meaningful) and is fragile under future reordering.
3. **Sort reactions canonically** at load time (e.g. by id) and
   regenerate the golden — accept one-time golden refresh as the
   migration cost.

Recommendation: option 3, with the regression suite's
`REGEN_GOLDEN=1` mechanism making the refresh a one-line operation.

## Trade-offs summary

| Aspect | Level 1 (planned) | Level 2 (proposed here) | Level 3 (SBML) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering effort | ~2 days | ~1–2 weeks | weeks |
| Byte-identical regression possible | yes | nearly (see above) | no (different solver) |
| Biologist edits a parameter | ✓ (TOML) | ✓ (TOML) | ✓ (any SBML editor) |
| Biologist adds a reaction | ✗ (Python) | ✓ (YAML) | ✓ (any SBML editor) |
| Biologist adds a new rate-law family | ✗ (Python) | ✗ (Python rate-law library) | ✗ (SBML extension, or custom function) |
| Add/swap parameter scenario | ✓ (`--params X.toml`) | ✓ (directory) | ✓ (load different SBML) |
| Use external tooling (COPASI, BioModels) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| New runtime dependency | none | none (PyYAML + tomllib) | libsbml + libsedml + tellurium |
| Custom rate laws stay in Python | yes | yes (rate-law library) | partial (SBML events / functions) |
| Debug surface | Python | Python + YAML | Python + YAML + SBML + RoadRunner |

## Recommendation

**Do Level 1, exactly as specified in §"Migration plan".** Defer Level
2 until either (a) the model gets a second user, or (b) the
dissertation lands and the planned parameter-tweak UI starts being
built (at which point Level 2 is the natural data layer underneath
it). Defer Level 3 indefinitely unless a specific interoperability
requirement appears.

The two paragraphs of dissertation-methods text this section
generates — "the implementation is currently parameter-externalised
(Level 1); reactions-as-data (Level 2) is designed but unimplemented,
preserving the option for the planned parameter-tweak UI to drive
both scenarios and topology from the same data layer" — are
themselves a contribution worth claiming. Architectural intent
documented and partially built > full implementation rushed under
deadline.

# References
- `reports/dissertation-notes.md §3.1` — `GAMMA_IP3R_S` calibration provenance
- `reports/lab-books/lab-book-2026-05-22-issue-44-45-dose-sweep.md` — today's session
- `models/platelet/tests/sim/test_byte_identical.py` — the regression suite
- Current data lives in:
  - `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/calcium_signalling.py`
  - `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/internal_state.py`
  - `reconstruction/platelet/dataclasses/process/resting_decay.py`
